textPlain: Interesting. When I first read the Picture of Dorian Gray, I had no idea of its social context -- I didn't know Oscar Wilde was gay, I didn't know the book was considered controversial. I just picked it up on a whim and thought it was a well-written book full of fantastically clever dialogue and over-the-top cynisism. But I was, ironically, left with an impression that the Victorian era was more comfortable with male intimacy, and appreciation for male beauty, than our own. That turned out not to be quite true, of course, as demonstrated by what they did to Wilde himself.But it's still a fantastic novel.
I just recently listened to this from Audible. I was a grand novel. The dialogue was amazing.
The longest lasting impact of the novel (personally for me) was how it introduced me to the concept of Opium Dens
Dorian Gray jokes never get old...
Except for that one written on the last page of the notebook that definitely did not age well.
